1: διὰ δεκατεσσάρων ἐτῶν (dia dekatessarōn etōn) — "after fourteen years." Whether reckoned from Paul's conversion or from the previous Jerusalem visit (1:18) is one of the most discussed chronological puzzles in Pauline scholarship, with significant implications for the dating of Galatians and the relationship to Acts 15. κατὰ ἀποκάλυψιν (kata apokalypsin) — "according to revelation" — the divine-revelation language of 1:12 reappears: even this deliberate consultation was prompted by a direct divine revelation, not human pressure or hierarchy. The consultation is not a concession to Jerusalem authority but a revelation-guided coordination.
2:
ἀνεθέμην αὐτοῖς τὸ εὐαγγέλιον ὃ κηρύσσω ἐν τοῖς ἔθνεσιν (anethemēn) — "I presented to them the gospel I proclaim among the Gentiles." Anatithēmi (to lay before, present for consideration) implies a collegial consultation, not a submission for approval: Paul laid out his gospel for mutual examination and understanding, not to receive authorization or endorsement of its content.

μή πως εἰς κενὸν τρέχω ἢ ἔδραμον — "lest somehow I was running or had run in vain." The athletic metaphor of running (trechō/trechein) in vain signals the practical concern: not that his gospel might be wrong, but that the two missions (circumcised and uncircumcised) might be working at cross-purposes in a way that would neutralize both.
3: οὐδὲ Τίτος ὁ σὺν ἐμοί, Ἕλλην ὤν, ἠναγκάσθη περιτμηθῆναι — "but even Titus, who was with me, was not compelled to be circumcised, even though he was a Greek." Titus is the test case: an uncircumcised Gentile believer physically present at the Jerusalem consultation, and not required to undergo circumcision. The aorist passive ēnankasthē (was not compelled) is the operative word — not that no one raised the issue, but that no compulsion was exerted. The pillar apostles' implicit approval of Titus's status is Paul's evidence that the Jerusalem leadership did not require circumcision as a condition of the Gentile mission.
4: διὰ δὲ τοὺς παρεισάκτους ψευδαδέλφους (pareisaktous pseudadelphous) — "because of the false brothers secretly brought in." Pareisagō (to bring in stealthily alongside) has the prefix para-eis- indicating infiltration: these individuals entered the gathering without proper standing. Pseudadelphos (false brother) is Paul's sharpest label — their claim of brotherhood is negated by their agenda. οἵτινες παρεισῆλθον κατασκοπῆσαι τὴν ἐλευθερίαν ἡμῶν — kataskopeo (to spy out, reconnoiter) is a military intelligence term; the intruders were scouts assessing the situation before a campaign to bring the Gentile mission under circumcision-requirement.
5: οἷς οὐδὲ πρὸς ὥραν εἴξαμεν τῇ ὑποταγῇ — "to whom we did not yield submission even for an hour." Eikaō (to yield, give way, capitulate) — the deliberate non-yielding: even temporary compliance would have been a concession in principle. "Not for an hour" is idiomatic emphasis: no partial accommodation, no provisional compliance pending clarification. The theological stake, stated in the purpose clause, is absolute: ἵνα ἡ ἀλήθεια τοῦ εὐαγγελίου διαμείνῃ πρὸς ὑμᾶς — "so that the truth of the gospel might remain with you" — the Galatians' own access to the gospel was at stake in Paul's resistance.
6: οἱ δοκοῦντες εἶναί τι (hoi dokountes einai ti) — "those who were considered to be something." The construction hoi dokountes (the reputed ones, those considered to be) is mildly ironic without being dismissive: Paul acknowledges the pillar apostles' reputation while refusing to let reputation function as theological authority. πρόσωπον θεὸς ἀνθρώπου οὐ λαμβάνει — "God shows no favoritism" — literally "God does not take the face of a person" (prosōpon... lambanō). This is the LXX idiom for partiality, translating Hebrew nasa panim (lift the face = show special favor). God's impartiality is the principle that subordinates reputation to truth.
7: πεπίστευμαι τὸ εὐαγγέλιον τῆς ἀκροβυστίας (pepisteumai) — "I have been entrusted with the gospel for the uncircumcised." The perfect passive pepisteumai (I have been entrusted and remain entrusted) marks the divine commissioning as completed and ongoing. The contrasting genitives — tēs akrobystias (of the uncircumcision) and tēs peritomēs (of the circumcision) — identify the target audiences, not two different gospels: the same gospel to two distinct mission fields.
8: ὁ γὰρ ἐνεργήσας Πέτρῳ εἰς ἀποστολὴν τῆς περιτομῆς ἐνήργησεν καὶ ἐμοί (energeō) — "the one who worked effectively in Peter for his apostleship to the circumcised worked effectively in me also." Energeō (to operate powerfully, work effectively) — the same verb in both clauses, emphasizing identical divine empowerment: the God who made Peter's apostolate effective made Paul's effective by the same means. The parallel structure places the two apostolicates in structural equivalence under the same divine agency — no hierarchy, no derivation of one from the other.
9: Ἰάκωβος καὶ Κηφᾶς καὶ Ἰωάννης, οἱ δοκοῦντες στῦλοι εἶναι (styloi) — "those reputed to be pillars." Styloi (columns, structural supports) is an architectural metaphor for those who bear the weight of the community: the pillars of the Jerusalem church. The order — James, Cephas, John — may reflect the authority structure of the early Jerusalem community, with James as its de facto president. δεξιὰς ἔδωκαν ἐμοὶ καὶ Βαρναβᾷ κοινωνίας — the right hand of fellowship (dexia koinōnias) was the formal gesture of recognized partnership and agreement in the Greco-Roman world: the pillar apostles acknowledged the Pauline mission as genuine and coordinate, not derivative.
10: μόνον τῶν πτωχῶν ἵνα μνημονεύωμεν — "only that we remember the poor." The single explicit condition of the Jerusalem agreement: material support for the poor of the Jerusalem community — probably the foundation of the collection that became a major Pauline project (1 Cor 16:1-4; 2 Cor 8-9; Rom 15:25-28). ὃ καὶ ἐσπούδασα αὐτὸ τοῦτο ποιῆσαι — "the very thing I was eager to do." Spoudazō (to be diligent, eager, make haste) — Paul embraced this obligation enthusiastically. The collection was not a burden imposed by Jerusalem but a chosen expression of the Gentile church's solidarity with the mother church.
11: κατὰ πρόσωπον αὐτῷ ἀντέστην (kata prosōpon autō antestēn) — "I opposed him to his face." Kata prosōpon (face to face, publicly) is the opposite of speaking behind someone's back; Paul confronted Peter directly and in front of the assembly. The incident probably belongs to the period described in Acts 15:35 — the interval after Paul and Barnabas's return to Antioch and before the second missionary journey. ὅτι κατεγνωσμένος ἦν — "because he stood condemned." Kataginōskō (to find fault, condemn, bring in a verdict against) in the perfect passive: the verdict against Peter's behavior was already rendered before Paul spoke — Paul's rebuke made public what was already true by the standard of the gospel.
12: πρὸ τοῦ γὰρ ἐλθεῖν τινας ἀπὸ Ἰακώβου μετὰ τῶν ἐθνῶν συνήσθιεν (synēsthien) — "before certain people came from James, he used to eat with the Gentiles." Synesthiō (to eat together with) — the table fellowship Peter maintained with Gentile believers was the practical expression of the equality-in-Christ that the gospel implied; sharing a meal was the strongest social bond available in the ancient world. ὅτε δὲ ἦλθον, ὑπέστελλεν καὶ ἀφώριζεν ἑαυτόν — hypostellō (to shrink back, withdraw) and aphorizō (to separate, set apart) — the Pharisaic separation vocabulary reenacted in the church; Peter re-enacted the wall the gospel had dismantled.
13: καὶ συνυπεκρίθησαν αὐτῷ καὶ οἱ λοιποὶ Ἰουδαῖοι (synypokrithēsan) — "and the rest of the Jewish believers joined him in his hypocrisy." Synypokrinomai (to act hypocritically alongside, play the same part together) — the theatrical image: actors performing a role that does not match their actual convictions. The compound verb (syn- = together) emphasizes the collective dimension: the hypocrisy was contagious and coordinated. ὥστε καὶ Βαρναβᾶς συναπήχθη αὐτῶν τῇ ὑποκρίσει — sunapagō (to lead away together with, be swept off course alongside) — even Paul's mission-partner was carried along by the social force of the crowd; the defection was not calculated but social-pressure-driven.
14:
οὐκ ὀρθοποδοῦσιν πρὸς τὴν ἀλήθειαν τοῦ εὐαγγελίου (orthopodousin) — "they were not walking in line with the truth of the gospel." Orthopodeō (to walk straight, walk with upright feet) — a hapax in Paul, probably coined from orthos (straight) + pous (foot): walking a straight line rather than a crooked one. The standard is not human propriety but "the truth of the gospel" — the gospel's own logic is the measure against which Peter's withdrawal was found deviant.

εἰ σύ, Ἰουδαῖος ὑπάρχων, ἐθνικῶς... ζῇς, πῶς τὰ ἔθνη ἀναγκάζεις ἰουδαΐζειν — the argument from Peter's own prior practice: if he himself had been living as a Gentile (without food-law separations), his withdrawal was not theological consistency but social fear. Ioudaizein (to Judaize, live in the Jewish manner) is the crux: Gentile believers were being effectively compelled to adopt Jewish practice as the hidden condition of full fellowship.
15: ἡμεῖς φύσει Ἰουδαῖοι καὶ οὐκ ἐξ ἐθνῶν ἁμαρτωλοί (physei Ioudaioi) — "we who are Jews by nature." Physei (by birth, by nature) marks the innate status as opposed to acquired status. "Gentile sinners" (ex ethnōn hamartoōloi) is the stock Jewish description of those outside the covenant — Gentiles were sinners by definition because they lived without Torah. Paul uses the phrase not to endorse its contempt for Gentiles but to work from a premise shared with his Jewish interlocutor: even those who began with Jewish birth-privilege have discovered that justification does not flow from that privilege.
16:
οὐ δικαιοῦται ἄνθρωπος ἐξ ἔργων νόμου ἐὰν μὴ διὰ πίστεως Ἰησοῦ Χριστοῦ — "a person is not justified by the works of the law but through the faith/faithfulness of Jesus Christ." Pisteos Iēsou Christou — the genitive is debated: "faith in Jesus Christ" (objective genitive: human faith directed toward Christ — the traditional reading) vs. "the faithfulness of Jesus Christ" (subjective genitive: Christ's own faithful obedience as the ground of justification). Paul's subsequent argument in 3:10-14 — where Christ actively "becomes a curse" on behalf of others — tilts toward the subjective reading as at least a contributing sense; but both are theologically coherent, and neither is to be excluded.

Ergōn nomou (works of the law) — the specific practices that marked Jewish covenant membership and maintained its boundaries (circumcision, food laws, Sabbath, festival observance), not "good works" in the general moral sense. The triple repetition of the phrase in this verse (ex ergōn nomou... ex ergōn nomou... ex ergōn nomou) underscores how central the debate over these boundary practices was to the Galatian crisis.
17: εἰ δὲ ζητοῦντες δικαιωθῆναι ἐν Χριστῷ εὑρέθημεν καὶ αὐτοὶ ἁμαρτωλοί, ἆρα Χριστὸς ἁμαρτίας διάκονος; μὴ γένοιτο — "if, in seeking to be justified in Christ, we ourselves also are found to be sinners, is Christ then a servant of sin? By no means!" The argument: if Jewish believers who have abandoned law-keeping for faith in Christ are thereby "sinners" (because they have transgressed the law's framework by eating with Gentiles), does Christ — the one who led them to that transgression — become sin's servant? Mē genoito (the strongest possible negative: may it never be!) categorically rejects the conclusion.
18: εἰ γὰρ ἃ κατέλυσα ταῦτα πάλιν οἰκοδομῶ, παραβάτην ἐμαυτὸν συνιστάνω — "for if I rebuild what I demolished, I prove myself to be a lawbreaker." The logic is pointed: Peter tore down the wall of separation by eating with Gentiles; by withdrawing, he rebuilt it. The rebuilding retroactively declares the original tearing-down to have been a transgression. Peter's withdrawal does not protect him from the accusation of lawbreaking — it constitutes a self-condemnation of his previous inclusive practice. The one who goes back establishes the wrongness of going forward.
19: ἐγὼ γὰρ διὰ νόμου νόμῳ ἀπέθανον ἵνα θεῷ ζήσω — "for through the law I died to the law so that I might live to God." The prepositions are carefully distinguished: dying dia nomou (through, by means of the law) and dying nomō (to the law, in relation to the law). The law's own logic — Deut 27:26's comprehensive curse; Lev 18:5's unattainable demand — drives the honest observer to recognize that the law cannot produce the righteousness it demands. The law becomes the instrument of its own undoing for Paul: following it to its conclusion arrives at death, and through that death comes freedom from its claim. Χριστῷ συνεσταύρωμαι — "I have been crucified with Christ" — perfect passive: a completed co-crucifixion with permanent present effects. The same compound as Rom 6:6; the death with Christ is both past event and present reality of identity.
20:
ζῶ δὲ οὐκέτι ἐγώ, ζῇ δὲ ἐν ἐμοὶ Χριστός — "it is no longer I who live, but Christ who lives in me." The displacement of the egō by the indwelling Christ is the positive consequence of co-crucifixion: the old self is put to death, but the one living now is not a vacuum but the Christ-inhabited new self. The present tense zō... zē (I live... Christ lives) describes a permanent present state, not a momentary experience.

ἐν πίστει ζῶ τῇ τοῦ υἱοῦ τοῦ θεοῦ τοῦ ἀγαπήσαντός με καὶ παραδόντος ἑαυτὸν ὑπὲρ ἐμοῦ — "I live by faith in the Son of God, who loved me and gave himself for me." The most intensely personal confession in Galatians: not "loved us" but "loved me" (agapēsantos me). The universal scope of the atonement is individualized in Paul's own experience; what Christ did for the world Paul claims as having been done for him specifically. This is not narcissism but the grammar of faith: the gospel must be received in the first person singular before it can be proclaimed in the first person plural.
21:
οὐκ ἀθετῶ τὴν χάριν τοῦ θεοῦ (atheteō) — "I do not nullify the grace of God." Atheteō (to set aside, declare void, render inoperative) is a legal term for nullifying a contract or reversing a verdict. To accept circumcision as a condition of justification would be to declare grace unnecessary — to nullify God's gift by claiming you do not need it.

εἰ γὰρ διὰ νόμου δικαιοσύνη, ἄρα Χριστὸς δωρεὰν ἀπέθανεν — "for if righteousness comes through the law, then Christ died for nothing." Dōrean (freely, without cause, to no purpose) — the same adverb as "hated me without cause" in Ps 69:4 / John 15:25. If law-keeping could produce righteousness, Christ's death was not merely unnecessary but gratuitous — an unjustified event in the cosmos. The logic cuts both ways: either Christ's death is necessary (because law-keeping cannot justify) or it was a meaningless waste.
